claim_text: Elite teams deploy multiple times per day.
For elite teams the lead time for changes is under an hour.
Continuous integration means merging working copies to a shared mainline several times a day.
In TDD the failing test is written before the implementation code.
The three pillars of observability are metrics, logs, and traces.
The incident commander coordinates the response during an active incident.
A blue-green deployment runs two identical production environments called blue and green.
The assured method provides bidirectional traceability between requirements, design, and tests.
The technical debt metaphor was coined by Ward Cunningham.
Pull requests under about four hundred lines help reviewers find more defects.
Elite teams restore service in well under sixty minutes after an outage.
Code coverage reports the proportion of source lines that the tests run.
Feature flags let teams release functionality separately from deploying code.
The change failure rate for the best teams sits at roughly fifteen percent.
Trunk-based development asks developers to commit small changes daily.
Elite teams achieve a deployment frequency of exactly forty-two per hour.
The test pyramid was invented in nineteen ninety-two by Kent Beck.
Pair programming requires three developers at a single keyboard.
Blue-green deployment was mandated by the ISO 9001 standard.
Semantic version numbers contain five separate components.
The solo method forbids using any version control system at all.
Observability guarantees one hundred percent uptime for every service.
